specific_location: Tamarack Ranch, western portion, near dirt road, about 1 km west of Highway 55 roughly 40.84 deg N, 102.82 deg W
description: The bird had the features that make spotted and eastern towhees distinctive: black head, bill, breast and wings; red eye; orange flanks; and white belly. The bird had a single white patch (check mark) on the wing, characteristic of eastern towhee, as opposed to numerous spots on the wings of spotted towhee. I also understand from consultation with Steve Mlodinow that the observed long white undertail spot (extending from the tip of the tail to nearly the vent) is a good indicator of easter towhee.Behaviors: The bird was perched and singing in a cottonwood tree for the minute or two that I observed it.Call: An unfamiliar song (the "drink your teeeee" song that I learned about after the sighting) attracted my attention to this bird. I also heard the familiar song of spotted towhee (few short notes followed by trill), perhaps from this bird and/or from spotted towhees in the same area. I don't recall hearing the eastern towhee call, but I was unfamiliar with it at the time.Plumage: adult
